images.alt:
The study surveyed 276 spring sites in western Himalayas, concentrated in Himachal Pradesh, found that 45% of the springs have completely dried up, while 26% have become semi-active i.e. from perennial spring to monsoon spring.It observed that 58 springs out of 117 springs in Chamba district, 21 out of 53 springs in LahaulSpiti, 17 out of 48 springs in Kullu, and 29 out of 58 springs in Kinnaur were found to have vanished or dried up.

The study pointed out that the region received rainfall decline by 100 mm to 250 mm especially in the monsoon season between June and August. Further, it was observed that maximum and minimum temperatures have increased in the past few decades. The region experienced an increase in maximum temperature by approximately 0.10°C to 0.20°C and minimum temperature increased by 0.15°C to 0.24°C per decade.

“The shortfall of rainfall and rising temperature has an impact on lateral water flow and groundwater levels which reduced spring water discharge and enhanced drying during the monsoon season” says lead author of the study Netrananda Sahu of Delhi School of Economics, University of Delhi.
